title: (1) Introduction
content_preview: The introduction outlines the structure of the answer, comparing political and constitutional crises in Pakistan from 1947-1956 and post-2008, and how they reflect governance weaknesses.

title: 2) 1947-1956: A State Without a Stable Political Compass
content_preview: This section examines the early crises in Pakistan from 1947 to 1956, marked by leadership vacuum after key figures' deaths, constitutional delays relying on the 1935 Act, arbitrary executive actions

title: a) Leadership Vacuum post independence
content_preview: Following Quaid-e-Azam's death in 1948 and Liaquat Ali Khan's assassination, Pakistan lacked stable leadership, with four prime ministers in four years, leaving the nation rudderless.

title: b) Constitutional Delay and Institutional Paralysis
content_preview: Pakistan operated without a permanent constitution for nine years, relying on the 1935 Government of India Act, leading to institutional paralysis.

title: c) Arbitrary Executive Authority Undermined Parliament
content_preview: Governor-General Ghulam Muhammad dismissed Prime Minister Nazimuddin in 1953 despite assembly support, exemplifying executive overreach.

title: d) Ethnic Disunity due to Forced Provincial Mergers
content_preview: The 1955 One Unit scheme merged West Pakistan provinces, sparking resentment in Balochistan and Sindh.

title: e) Judiciary Enabled Undemocratic Rule
content_preview: In the 1955 Tamizuddin Case, the Federal Court validated the dissolution of the Constituent Assembly by an unelected Governor-General.

title: 3) Post 2008: A Democracy Captured by Power Struggles and Legal Battles
content_preview: This section analyzes post-2008 crises, including civil-military tensions like the 2011 Memogate scandal, judicial overreach disqualifying PM Nawaz Sharif in 2017 over the Panama leaks, weak coalition

title: (i) Civil-Military Conflict behind Democratic Facade
content_preview: The 2011 Memogate scandal revealed civilian fears of military coups following Osama bin Laden's killing.

title: (ii) Judicial Overreach into Political Domain
content_preview: In 2017, PM Nawaz Sharif was disqualified over a minor iqama issue from the Panama leaks, seen as politically motivated.

title: (iii) Weak and Divided Coalition Governments
content_preview: The 2022 PDM alliance faced internal contradictions, hindering consistent governance and reforms.

title: iv) Politicized Accountability Undermines Rule of Law
content_preview: NAB disproportionately arrested opposition leaders during PTI rule, criticized by HRCP in 2020.

title: V) Constitutional Conflict between Judiciary and Executive
content_preview: The 2023 deadlock over Punjab and KP elections highlighted failures to enforce constitutional timelines under Article 224 due to political interference.

title: (4) Broader Structural Weaknesses in Governance Across Both Eras
content_preview: Common weaknesses include lack of institutional continuity with frequent prime ministerial changes, absence of checks and balances through excessive ordinances, overdependence on unelected institution

title: a) Lack of Institutional Continuity
content_preview: Frequent weak parliamentary roots and regime changes, with 14 prime ministers from 1947 to 2025, undermine political sustainability.

title: b) Absence of Checks and Balances
content_preview: Leaders from Ghulam Muhammad to Imran Khan excessively used ordinances, bypassing legislative processes.

title: (c) Overdependence on Unelected Institutions
content_preview: The military's excessive influence over civil decisions, such as ISPR press conferences, shapes political narratives.

title: d) Weak Local Government System
content_preview: Grassroots democracy is ignored despite constitutional provisions; for example, no local government elections were held in Punjab from 2016 to 2022.

title: 5- Critical Appraisal
content_preview: This section provides a critical evaluation of the structural weaknesses and their persistence across eras.

title: 6. Conclusion
content_preview: The conclusion summarizes how these crises reflect enduring governance flaws in Pakistan, emphasizing the need for institutional reforms.
